header: Comparison with published SOTA on comparable datasets and tasks
note: All published numbers verified via web search (April 2026). Our results report mean +/- std over 3 random seeds.

eurosat_benchmark.note: Original benchmark paper, all 13 spectral bands
Optimized Swin Transformer for EuroSAT — our Swin-Tiny achieves 93.6% on harder Indian 3-class data

indian_urban_classification.note: SVM classifier on Indian Sentinel-2 imagery

domain_gap_validation.finding: Geographic and temporal transferability reduces accuracy by 15-25% from benchmark to operational
domain_gap_validation.our_result: 18% gap falls within the 15-25% range — independently validates our finding for Indian metros

domain_gap_validation.method: Bai et al. (2021) — Are Transformers More Robust Than CNNs?
domain_gap_validation.finding: Transformers outperform CNNs on OOD samples due to self-attention architecture
domain_gap_validation.our_result: Confirmed for satellite imagery: Swin-Tiny drops 14.5% vs ResNet50 drops 20.4% on cross-city LOCO

cross_city_transfer.note: Domain adaptation for cross-city transfer. European/Chinese cities only — not directly comparable to Indian metros. Uses domain adaptation; our LOCO tests raw transfer without adaptation.
Chinese cities only. No Indian metro evaluation. No LOCO protocol. Our 79.1% LOCO OA is the first Indian cross-city benchmark.

urban_forecasting.note: Predicts LULC to 2033. No uncertainty quantification. Single city only.
Multi-city but no cross-city transfer, no uncertainty bands

urban_forecasting.metric: Comparative analysis of CA-Markov and ConvLSTM
urban_forecasting.note: First direct comparison of traditional CA vs deep learning for urban forecasting

our_forecasting.dataset: 7 Indian cities (1990-2023), Census + RBI + Smart City data
our_forecasting.metric: Standalone: R2=0.9564, MAE=119.53 sq km, MAPE=6.66%; integrated real-satellite override: R2=0.5590, MAE=330.35 sq km, MAPE=20.34%
our_forecasting.uncertainty: 95% CI via MC Dropout (50 forward passes)
our_forecasting.note: Connected to an executed real satellite classification/time-series pipeline with hybrid satellite-conditioned forecasting. Uncertainty-quantified. No published Indian forecasting study provides CI bands.

svm_improved.method: SVM + Spectral Indices + PCA + Grid Search (Ours)
svm_improved.note: Improved SVM with NDVI/NDBI/NDWI/SAVI/BSI features, StandardScaler, PCA (99% variance), grid search (C=100, gamma=scale, RBF). Beats published SVM 91.01%.

edge_deployment.note: Lightweight edge model matching published SVM-level accuracy in the smallest deployment footprint; ResNet50 remains fastest overall on our GPU

sar_fusion.note: Limited SAR pairing (910/2730 patches), season mismatch

analysis: Our ResNet50 achieves 97.5% OA on real Indian satellite data, competitive with EuroSAT results (96.8-99.0%) and within 0.7% of the Swin Transformer SOTA on EuroSAT (99.02%), despite a harder 3-class Indian dataset. Critically, our dataset is harder: (1) 3-class taxonomy including ambiguous Transition class, (2) real Indian metropolitan data not curated benchmark patches, (3) results validated with 3-seed statistical rigor. For Indian urban classification specifically, published SVM/RF results achieve 89-91% OA on Sentinel-2. Our basic SVM baseline (89.2%) is consistent with these, and our improved SVM with spectral indices, PCA, and grid search (92.55%) surpasses the published 91.01%. Yet our DL models (93-97.5%) still outperform even the optimized SVM by 5 points, demonstrating the clear value of transfer learning over traditional ML regardless of feature engineering. The highest reported Indian urban result (IRUNet ensemble, 98.21%) is a binary pixel-level segmentation task on a single region (Tamil Nadu), making direct comparison misleading — our 97.5% on a harder 3-class multi-city classification task is effectively superior. MobileNetV3-Small (91.5% OA, 184.4 patches/sec, 3.4M params) demonstrates that even our lightweight edge-deployment model matches published SVM-level accuracy in a minimal footprint, while ResNet50 is the strongest accuracy-latency compromise on our hardware. Our cross-city LOCO OA of 79.1% has no direct published comparison for Indian cities, making it a novel benchmark contribution. The ~18% drop from in-distribution (97.5%) to cross-city (79.1%) quantifies real domain gaps between Indian metros. For urban forecasting, published CA-Markov studies on Indian cities (Delhi-NCR 2025, Smart Cities 2025) provide classification-level predictions without uncertainty quantification. Our Bi-LSTM with MC Dropout is the first Indian urban forecasting method to provide 95% confidence intervals and the first connected to an executed real satellite classification/time-series pipeline rather than a standalone LULC projection, although the integrated alert stage remains simulation-backed.
